skill.name: azure-cost-optimization
skill.description: Analyze Azure subscription costs, utilization metrics, and Redis resources to identify savings opportunities and produce approval-ready optimization reports.
skill.summary: Find Azure cost savings with actual cost data, usage metrics, and prioritized recommendations.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are mainly markdown examples for Azure CLI, PowerShell, Azure MCP tools, KQL, and report templates. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscated code, or malicious control flow was found. The skill remains medium risk because it guides authenticated Azure cost queries, writes local audit files, fetches official pricing pages, and may generate destructive cleanup commands that require explicit approval.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Azure Operations Require User Oversight
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Azure CLI, PowerShell, Azure MCP, and REST commands that run against authenticated Azure subscriptions. This is legitimate for cost analysis, but it can expose subscription metadata and affect cloud resources if executed without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and semantically tied to Azure cost analysis. They are not hidden or obfuscated, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Cleanup Commands Are Present as Recommendations
medium_findings.description: The Redis guidance includes delete operations for failed or unused caches and the main skill includes temporary folder deletion. The skill also states that destructive operations require explicit approval, which reduces but does not remove the risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains clear delete guidance, but it is presented as user-approved cloud cleanup rather than automatic execution. The approval requirement lowers confidence that this is a security vulnerability.

medium_findings.title: Local Audit Files May Contain Sensitive Cost Metadata
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to save cost query results and optimization reports in local output files. These files can contain resource IDs, subscription identifiers, costs, owner tags, and operational recommendations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The report-writing behavior is explicit and expected for auditability. The sensitivity depends on the user environment and storage controls.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography hits occur on descriptive text, YAML list items, KQL examples, or safety guidance. Review found no code that computes weak hashes, disables TLS, or uses obsolete cryptographic primitives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prose or query snippets, not cryptographic implementations. The only explicit cryptographic guidance found recommends TLS 1.2 minimum in the SDK reference.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Matches Are Relative Markdown Links
low_findings.description: The two filesystem path traversal hits are relative links from the Redis reference to local report templates. They do not read arbitrary files, accept user-controlled paths, or escape a runtime sandbox.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both instances are static markdown links to repository templates. There is no file access API or runtime path construction.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Point to Official Azure Services
low_findings.description: The network findings are official Azure Management, Azure Portal, and Azure pricing URLs used to query costs and validate prices. No evidence found of third-party callbacks, credential exfiltration, or hidden tracking endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains and paths are visible and match normal Azure cost-management workflows. The risk is disclosure through expected Azure API usage, not malicious network behavior.

low_findings.title: C2 Keyword Match Is a Redis SKU Name
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword hit is a Redis cache SKU named Standard C2 in an example report. It does not indicate command-and-control behavior or malware infrastructure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact context is a Redis capacity tier in a cost report. No network beaconing, remote-control behavior, or suspicious endpoint is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: PowerShell and Azure CLI Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes command examples for Azure account discovery, resource listing, cost queries, metrics queries, azqr scans, Redis operations, and local cleanup. These are legitimate examples but should run only with explicit user intent and least-privilege Azure roles.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The patterns are numerous and explicit, but all reviewed instances are documentation for Azure administration. No automatic execution wrapper was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Official Azure REST and Pricing Fetches
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill calls the Azure Management API and official Azure pricing pages. Users should understand that resource, cost, and subscription details may be sent to Azure services during normal operation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are explicit and official. The data exposure risk is real but expected for Azure cost-management workflows.

content.value_statement: Azure teams often lack clear proof of where cloud spending can be reduced. This skill combines cost data, utilization metrics, Azure Quick Review results, and Redis-specific checks into prioritized savings recommendations.
actual_capabilities: Checks Azure CLI, Azure MCP, and azqr prerequisites before analysis.
Runs Azure Quick Review guidance to identify orphaned and cost-impacting resources.
Queries Azure Cost Management for actual cost data over a defined period.
Uses Azure Monitor metrics to support rightsizing recommendations.
Provides Redis-specific cost rules, report templates, and subscription scope options.
Generates audit-ready reports with data sources, savings estimates, and implementation notes.
limitations: Requires authenticated Azure access and reader-level permissions for target subscriptions.
Savings estimates depend on current pricing, utilization windows, and resource tags.
The skill does not deploy fixes or delete resources without user approval.
It focuses on cost optimization, not security diagnostics or general incident response.

use_cases.title: Monthly FinOps Review
use_cases.description: Review actual Azure costs, top cost drivers, orphaned resources, and validated savings opportunities for a monthly optimization cycle.

use_cases.title: Engineering Rightsizing Plan
use_cases.description: Combine Azure Monitor metrics with pricing data to plan safe VM, app service, storage, and Redis rightsizing changes.

use_cases.title: Redis Cost Cleanup
use_cases.description: Analyze Redis caches across selected subscriptions and flag failed, oversized, untagged, or long-running test instances.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Cost Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure subscription for cost optimization. First check prerequisites, then ask me for the subscription scope before running any commands.

prompt_templates.title: Find Immediate Savings
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze orphaned and unused Azure resources in this subscription. Use Azure Quick Review and Resource Graph patterns, then rank savings by risk and effort.

prompt_templates.title: Rightsize With Metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Use actual cost data and the last 14 days of utilization metrics to recommend rightsizing options for expensive Azure resources.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Redis Spending
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a Redis-specific cost optimization analysis across subscriptions with this prefix. Produce a detailed report with cache-level recommendations and approval gates.

output_examples.input: Analyze subscription costs for the last 30 days.
output_examples.output: A report that lists total monthly cost, top resources by spend, free-tier usage, orphaned resources, and prioritized savings recommendations.

output_examples.input: Optimize Redis costs for development subscriptions.
output_examples.output: A Redis summary that flags failed caches, premium tiers in non-production, missing expiration tags, and estimated monthly savings.

output_examples.input: Create an implementation plan for the recommended changes.
output_examples.output: A staged action plan with owner checks, approval requirements, validation steps, rollback notes, and expected monthly savings.

best_practices: Use least-privilege Azure roles and confirm the subscription scope before collecting cost data.
Validate recommendations with actual costs, utilization metrics, and official Azure pricing.
Get explicit approval before deleting resources or applying rightsizing changes.
anti_patterns: Do not estimate savings without querying Azure Cost Management or current pricing.
Do not delete failed or unused resources until the owner and environment are confirmed.
Do not store generated reports where unauthorized users can read cost or resource metadata.

faq.question: What Azure permissions are required?
faq.answer: The skill expects Cost Management Reader, Monitoring Reader, and Reader access for the selected subscription or resource group.

faq.question: Can it make changes to Azure resources?
faq.answer: It can generate commands and recommendations, but destructive actions require explicit user approval before execution.

faq.question: Does it support Redis-specific optimization?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Redis rules for failed caches, oversized development tiers, old test caches, missing tags, and subscription filtering.

faq.question: How does it estimate savings?
faq.answer: It combines actual Azure cost data, utilization metrics, Azure Quick Review findings, and current Azure pricing references.

faq.question: Where are reports saved?
faq.answer: The skill instructs agents to save cost reports and query results in the local output folder with timestamps.

faq.question: Is it suitable for security audits?
faq.answer: No. The skill is focused on cost optimization. Use a dedicated Azure security skill for security diagnostics.
